research_series.series_name: HPA Axis, Cortisol, Stress Physiology, Fatigue, Anxiety, Mood, and Cognition

patterns.pattern_name: Flattened diurnal cortisol slope or blunted CAR is associated with fatigue, burnout, and exhaustion

mediator.name: flattened_diurnal_cortisol_slope_or_blunted_CAR
mediator.pathway: chronic stress/allostatic load altering circadian HPA-axis rhythm
mediator.mechanism: reduced dynamic cortisol rise or flattened daily rhythm may reflect impaired stress-system flexibility

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Flattened diurnal cortisol slope or blunted cortisol awakening response increases the likelihood of fatigue, burnout, or exhaustion, especially in chronic stress/fatigue populations.

hypotheses.reason: Systematic reviews/meta-analyses support associations between within-day cortisol rhythm abnormalities and fatigue/burnout/CFS outcomes.

evidence.summary: Within-day cortisol dynamics are more informative than total daily cortisol output for fatigue and burnout states.

key_sources.title: Unstimulated cortisol secretory activity in everyday life and its relationship with fatigue and chronic fatigue syndrome
key_sources.relevant_finding: CFS shows attenuated CAR increase; within-day change markers most relevant to fatigue experience.

limitations: Total cortisol output is less reliable than rhythm measures
alternative_explanations: Sleep disruption may drive both cortisol rhythm and fatigue
Reverse causality from exhaustion to altered routines

patterns.pattern_name: Acute psychological stress increases cortisol and reduces HRV/vagal tone

mediator.name: cortisol_stress_reactivity_and_parasympathetic_withdrawal
mediator.pathway: acute stress activates HPA axis and autonomic stress response
mediator.mechanism: coordinated cortisol secretion with reduced vagal modulation during stress response

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Acute psychological stress commonly produces increased cortisol and reduced vagally mediated HRV.

hypotheses.reason: Lab stress/TSST studies using simultaneous cortisol and ECG-derived HRV show this coordinated response.

evidence.summary: Acute stress reliably increases cortisol and lowers HRV/vagal tone, but baseline cortisol-HRV correlations are often weak.

key_sources.title: HPA-axis and inflammatory reactivity to acute stress is related with basal HPA-axis activity
key_sources.relevant_finding: Acute stress reactivity involves HPA-axis and inflammatory changes.

limitations: Stress task ecology may differ from real life
Cortisol and HRV timing windows differ
Baseline HRV-cortisol association is not strong

patterns.pattern_name: Chronic stress/allostatic load predicts inflammatory and metabolic dysregulation via altered HPA-axis patterns

mediator.name: altered_HPA_axis_pattern
mediator.mechanism: long-term stress-system dysregulation interacts with inflammatory and metabolic pathways

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Chronic stress and allostatic load are associated with altered cortisol rhythms and greater inflammatory/metabolic risk.

hypotheses.reason: Large cohort and systematic-review evidence links chronic cortisol rhythm alterations to CRP/IL-6/glucose dysregulation and mood/fatigue outcomes.

evidence.summary: Evidence supports chronic stress/allostatic load associations with inflammatory/metabolic dysregulation, but full mediation chains need more direct testing.

key_sources.title: Towards a consensus definition of allostatic load
key_sources.relevant_finding: Allostatic load requires multi-system, multi-biomarker modeling.

alternative_explanations: Poor sleep and lifestyle mediate both stress and biomarkers

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Baseline HRV and cortisol are not reliably correlated under resting conditions.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: The report notes weak or absent baseline associations except under anticipation or chronic adversity contexts.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Total daily cortisol output is not a reliable predictor of cognitive performance or fatigue outside clinical extremes.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Within-day cortisol pattern markers outperform total output; cognitive links are mixed.

high_value_candidate_nodes.definition: Reduced decline in cortisol across the day, often interpreted as altered circadian HPA-axis regulation.
Reduced normal rise in cortisol after awakening.
Cortisol increase after acute psychosocial stress exposure.
Multi-system cumulative physiological burden from chronic adaptation to stressors.

research_gaps: Mediation analyses from chronic/adverse exposures to HPA-axis pattern to downstream neurobehavioral outcome
Mechanistic pathways linking circadian misalignment/HPA dysregulation to metabolic/inflammatory disease risk
Longitudinal cognition outcomes with cortisol pattern measures

most_supported_patterns: Flattened diurnal cortisol slope/blunted CAR associates with fatigue/burnout/exhaustion
Acute psychological stress increases cortisol and reduces HRV/vagal tone
highest_value_patterns_for_inference: Within-day cortisol rhythm markers are more useful than total cortisol output
Acute stress should be modeled as coordinated endocrine-autonomic response
patterns_requiring_caution: Total cortisol output as cognition/fatigue predictor
recommended_next_queries: How do inflammatory cytokines and sickness behavior mediate fatigue, mood, and cognition in humans?
How do CRP, IL-6, TNF-alpha, and acute infection/inflammation affect subjective fatigue and cognitive performance?
summary.overall_assessment: HPA/cortisol should be represented as pattern-specific rhythm and reactivity nodes, not as a generic cortisol_level node. Strongest support is for flattened/blunted within-day rhythms in fatigue/burnout and for acute stress causing coordinated cortisol increase with vagal withdrawal.

works_cited.title: Unstimulated cortisol secretory activity in everyday life and its relationship with fatigue and chronic fatigue syndrome
HPA-axis and inflammatory reactivity to acute stress is related with basal HPA-axis activity
Towards a consensus definition of allostatic load
